turns.content: I'm reading a blog about sqlx and I thought the migration files would be separate binaries or external scripts, but this post mentions running migrations at startup using some kind of macro. Is there actually a way to embed the migration logic directly into the crate?

You are right about the macro part; it is the sqlx::migrate! macro that handles this. It allows you to define migrations inside your Rust code as a collection of closure functions or structs rather than separate files, and then call the macro to register them for execution at startup.

That sounds great but I'm worried about performance. If I just run the migrations every time the server starts, won't that be slow? My team's main concern is avoiding that latency during high-traffic periods.

You are correct to worry about that; running a full schema migration on every request would indeed cause significant latency. The solution is to make migrations idempotent and run them only once per process restart, typically using the Database trait connection pool to ensure they execute exactly one time without repeating.
Okay so I just need to add sqlx::migrate! macro to my main module? But wait, what if someone manually runs migrations outside of the application? Won't that cause duplicate tables?

That is a very sharp point. You are correct; the sqlx::migrate! macro does not automatically prevent manual execution or guarantee uniqueness across different environments. The recommended pattern is to wrap your migration logic in a trait bound that checks if the table already exists before running any SQL statements, ensuring safety even if migrations are run externally.

I'm studying for my interview and trying to understand how sqlx::migrate! handles embedded migrations in Rust. Imagine I have a crate with a migration file stored as a string literal, but when I run the tooling at startup it fails because the schema hasn't been applied yet.

The common mistake is trying to execute sqlx::migrate! on an empty database or assuming the migration files are automatically available without proper setup. The tooling expects a specific directory structure with versioned folders, and if you don't initialize the migrations table first using the builder pattern or a separate bootstrap script, the runtime will fail because no rows exist to update. You need to ensure the migrations table exists before running sqlx::migrate!.

Ah, so I thought just embedding the migration string in my code was enough without worrying about the database state. But you mentioned initialization order is critical here. Does that mean I need to manually create a table or can sqlx handle it automatically if I configure something specific?

You don't necessarily have to manually create every intermediate table, but the migrations table itself must exist before any migration runs. If you are using a feature flag like enable_migrations in your configuration or ensuring the builder has been properly initialized with the correct connection pool and driver settings, sqlx will attempt to run the migrations against an empty database which is where the error occurs. The core issue is that sqlx::migrate! assumes a valid schema already exists unless explicitly told otherwise through initialization flags.

Why does sqlx::migrate! even work this way with embedded migration files? I thought I had to manually write the schema and call a separate tool, but here I am just calling a macro and it handles everything. It feels like magic compared to how my university database class taught us to run DDL scripts on the command line.

That frustration is very common when transitioning from academic SQL syntax to Rust's procedural macros. The macro sqlx::migrate! takes a string slice containing your migration definition and uses compile-time reflection to parse it into a concrete type that matches the database driver's expectations. By embedding migrations in source code as strings, you avoid external dependencies like Flyway or Liquibase entirely, which means your application only needs to compile Rust to run its schema changes at startup.

But I still don't get how it connects to what we learned about schema evolution. If I change a column type in my Rust code and recompile, does the macro automatically detect that migration file needs updating? Or do I have to manually edit the string inside the macro?

You don't need to manually track every change; the system treats your Rust source code as the single source of truth for the schema. When you modify a migration definition within the sqlx::migrate! macro, the build process generates a new set of migration files based on that specific string representation. This means if you update the column type in your Rust logic or change the migration string, recompiling the crate automatically produces updated migration artifacts, ensuring your database schema always matches your application's intended design without manual file management.
